metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify, in the `--report` metric definitions, how firing rows with `decision == 'indeterminate'` are handled (e.g., excluded from both numerator and denominator of all metrics, same as overflow), and add an AC4 sub-assertion that covers this case.
evidence: Plan defines blocking fix-units as those where `decision == 'block'` and FP proxy as those where `decision == 'dropped'`, but never addresses `decision == 'indeterminate'`.

sidecar.py:472-478: `all_findings = verdict.get('blocking', []) + verdict.get('advisory', []) + verdict.get('overflow', []) + verdict.get('indeterminate', []) + verdict.get('dropped', [])` — all five decision categories are persisted into findings[].
orchestrator.py:492,501: INDETERMINATE verdict findings are a real population that will appear in sidecar findings with decision='indeterminate'.
AC4(b) asks to assert a blocking fix-unit 'floor-suppressed' (drop_reason set) — but if an indeterminate finding is mistakenly counted as blocking, the denominator is wrong.
findings.finding: The plan's detection_proxy and blocking_fp_proxy computation rules reference `decision == 'block'` and `decision == 'dropped'` but never specify how a firing row with `decision == 'indeterminate'` is handled — excluded silently, counted as blocking, or flagged. The per-finding decision vocabulary confirmed in the codebase (orchestrator.py:404,417,492,495-503; sidecar.py:302-311) includes 'indeterminate' as a valid persisted value, and all findings (including indeterminate) are included in the sidecar's `findings[]` list. An implementer writing the recorder's metric logic has no rule for this case.
findings.location: What this delivers — section 2 (`--report`), `detection_proxy` and `blocking_fp_proxy` definitions; also AC4
scenarios: Implementer includes indeterminate findings in blocking fix-unit denominator, inflating it and deflating both proxies.
Implementer excludes indeterminate findings entirely without documentation, making the metric silently incomplete for criteria that frequently trigger INDETERMINATE (LLM-unavailable path).
AC4 unit tests pass on clean synthetic fixtures but the production ledger silently miscounts a real corpus with INDETERMINATE rounds.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the detection_proxy / blocking_fp_proxy definitions: 'Firings with decision `indeterminate` or `overflow` (i.e., decision not in {"block", "advisory", "dropped"}) are excluded from all metric denominators and numerators.' Then add AC4(f): a criterion with only indeterminate firings yields null for both proxies.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state the multi-criteria attribution rule for fix-unit denominators: whether a firing row with `c=['T1','T2']` contributes the same fix-unit to BOTH T1 and T2 independently (fan-out), and add an AC4 sub-assertion covering a fixture where a single fix-unit is co-cited by two criteria.
evidence: Plan: 'fix-units are attributed to the criterion via the firing's c criteria list' — no fan-out vs exclusive attribution rule stated.
Plan row schema: `c`=criteria (list) — a single finding can be cited by N criteria simultaneously (this is the whole point of the co-citation analysis mentioned in the sidecar cohort field).
sidecar.py:420: `'criteria': f.get('criteria', [])` — criteria is a list persisted per finding.

The plan explicitly says fix_unit_key is CRITERIA-FREE (sidecar.py:279-290), meaning one physical defect can appear in multiple criteria's firing rows — the attribution rule determines the denominator per criterion.
findings.finding: The multi-criteria attribution rule for blocking fix-unit counting is not co-located with the `detection_proxy` and `blocking_fp_proxy` definitions. The plan states each firing row carries `c`=criteria list (a multi-valued list), and fix-units are 'attributed to the criterion via the firing's c criteria list.' It never specifies whether a single firing row with `c=['T1','T2']` contributes the same fix-unit to BOTH T1's and T2's denominators independently (fan-out) or is attributed only once per the primary criterion. This is a load-bearing decision rule: fan-out causes one remediated defect to count toward multiple criteria simultaneously, which can inflate `detection_proxy` symmetrically across co-cited criteria.
findings.location: What this delivers — section 2 (`--report`), `detection_proxy` and `blocking_fp_proxy` definitions; Recorder section (`fix_unit_key` attribution description)
scenarios: A defect co-cited by T1 and T2 is remediated. With fan-out attribution it counts in BOTH T1 and T2 detection_proxy numerators and denominators — which may be correct but is never stated.
An implementer uses exclusive attribution (only count the fix-unit for the first criterion in c[]) and gets a different metric from one using fan-out — both are reasonable readings of the plan.
AC4 unit tests use single-criterion fixtures and never expose the ambiguity; the production corpus has multi-criterion findings that trigger divergent behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the --report metric definitions: 'A fixing row with c=["T1","T2"] contributes the same fix_unit_key to each named criterion's denominator independently (fan-out); a single remediated defect can increment the detection_proxy numerator for every criterion that cited it.' Then add AC4(g): a fix-unit with c=["T1","T2"] appears in both criteria's sample_counts.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'Reused existing symbols' list: remove `sidecar.all_review_results` (the recorder cannot call it — it drops the envelope) and add the actual dependencies needed for direct file enumeration (`rebar.config.tracker_dir`, `rebar._engine_support.resolver.resolve_ticket_id`) or clarify that the recorder re-implements the enumeration inline without calling any `all_review_results` API.
evidence: Plan: 'Reused existing symbols (verified present). rebar.llm.plan_review.sidecar.all_review_results …'

Plan simultaneously: 'the recorder mirrors all_review_results's file-enumeration + v1/v2 schema guard but reads the filename directly, because all_review_results returns only the payload data body and drops the event envelope (so ts/uuid are NOT available from it)'
sidecar.py lines 197-202: `payload = event.get('data') if isinstance(event, dict) else None` — envelope ts/uuid are never returned by all_review_results

If the recorder must NOT call all_review_results to get ts/uuid, it cannot 'reuse' that symbol at all — only rebar.config.tracker_dir and resolve_ticket_id (used internally by all_review_results) would need to be reused for direct file enumeration
findings.finding: The plan lists `sidecar.all_review_results` as a 'Reused existing symbol' but the recorder's own described design explicitly CANNOT call it — the recorder must enumerate ticket directories and read filenames directly to obtain ts_ns and uuid, which all_review_results drops. The function is not reused; only its internal enumeration pattern is mirrored. This false-reuse claim could mislead the implementer into attempting to call all_review_results and then finding the envelope data missing.
findings.location: Scope — 'Reused existing symbols (verified present)' section; also 'What this delivers' item 1 paragraph ('the recorder mirrors all_review_results's file-enumeration … but reads the filename directly')
scenarios: Implementer reads the 'Reused existing symbols' list, calls sidecar.all_review_results(), receives only payload dicts with no ts or uuid fields, then must discover they need to re-implement the enumeration from scratch — wasting time or shipping a recorder that silently omits the envelope identity.

The 'Reused existing symbols' list serves as an implementation contract; a false entry there can cause scope omissions (the needed symbols — tracker_dir, resolve_ticket_id, os.listdir — aren't listed).
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'sidecar.all_review_results' in the reused-symbols list with: 'sidecar.all_review_results (pattern mirrored but NOT called — the recorder enumerates ticket dirs directly via rebar.config.tracker_dir + resolve_ticket_id to preserve filename ts_ns/uuid).' Also add tracker_dir and resolve_ticket_id to the reused symbols.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `--backfill` discovers the full set of ticket IDs to walk (e.g., list all subdirectories of the tracker dir whose names match the ticket-id pattern and contain at least one `*-REVIEW_RESULT.json` file), so the recorder's enumeration step is unambiguous.
evidence: Plan: 'the recorder mirrors all_review_results's file-enumeration + v1/v2 schema guard but reads the filename directly, because all_review_results returns only the payload data body'
sidecar.all_review_results signature: `def all_review_results(ticket_id: str, *, repo_root=None)` — takes ONE ticket_id, does not enumerate tickets
No statement in the plan of how the full set of ticket IDs is discovered for --backfill
ABSENCE: no mention of tracker-dir listing, ticket index, or any enumeration API in the scope or implementation description
findings.finding: The recorder's --backfill (and --record) mode must enumerate ALL ticket IDs that have REVIEW_RESULT sidecars, but the plan never specifies how the harness discovers this set. sidecar.all_review_results(ticket_id) takes a single ticket_id; the plan's direct-file-read strategy requires knowing which ticket directories to walk first. No enumeration mechanism (e.g., listing tracker subdirectories, reading from an index, or iterating the .tickets-tracker/ tree) is named in the plan or scope.
findings.location: Scope / What this delivers §1 (Recorder) — 'reads the persisted REVIEW_RESULT sidecars' and AC1 proving command
scenarios: --backfill runs but has no ticket IDs to iterate over, silently producing an empty ledger and making AC1's >5000 line count assertion fail

Implementer must invent the enumeration step independently, risking inconsistency with how gate_eval_instrumentation.py discovers tickets (which uses the outcome_corpus.jsonl miner, not available here)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Recorder section: 'Ticket enumeration: list all immediate subdirectories of the tracker dir (resolved via `rebar.config.tracker_dir()`), filter to those containing at least one file matching `*-REVIEW_RESULT.json`, and process each as a ticket_id. This mirrors how `sidecar.all_review_results` resolves the ticket dir but lifts the outer loop to the harness.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (and in the AC6 doc section scope) at least draft promotion-gate thresholds — e.g., minimum `detection_proxy`, maximum `blocking_fp_proxy`, minimum `sample_counts.blocking_fix_units`, and observation window — so the section R1/R3/R4 reference contains actionable criteria, not just a description of the recorder.
evidence: 'docs/plan-review-gate.md gains a section documenting the standing recorder, the advisory→blocking promotion gate that R1/R3/R4 reference'
AC6 prove: `grep -q "criterion_effectiveness" docs/plan-review-gate.md && grep -q "promotion" docs/plan-review-gate.md`
ABSENCE: no numeric thresholds or decision criteria for promotion specified anywhere in the plan
'It complements R7 (b1a4, already landed) and is referenced by R1/R3/R4's advisory criteria via auto-inclusion'
findings.finding: The advisory→blocking promotion-gate protocol is referenced by R1, R3, and R4 as a dependency, but the plan's AC6 proof command only checks for the presence of the strings 'criterion_effectiveness' and 'promotion' in `docs/plan-review-gate.md` — it does not ensure the section contains actionable promotion thresholds (e.g., minimum detection_proxy, maximum blocking_fp_proxy, minimum sample count, observation window) that the referencing epics can actually act on.
findings.location: AC6; What this delivers §2: `--report`; 'Wiring / cadence' section
scenarios: R1/R3/R4 ship advisory criteria and look to `docs/plan-review-gate.md` for promotion guidance, but find only a narrative description with no actionable thresholds — the gate exists in name only
A gate maintainer must decide whether to promote a criterion but has no stated criteria to apply, leading to ad-hoc, inconsistent decisions

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a schema-version field (e.g., `"_v": 1`) to every emitted ledger row and add a guard in `--report` that errors or warns on unrecognized schema versions, so future schema evolution is detectable.
evidence: 'Each row uses short keys (compact + jq-friendly), documented in the harness docstring'
No schema version field mentioned in the row spec (`t`, `ts`, `r`, `v`, `c`, `n`, `u`, `d`, `s`, `p`, `x`)
'The ledger is prune-immune because the sidecar's own retention prune caps history at RETAIN_PER_TICKET=50 rounds/ticket — a durable firing ledger captures firings before that prune'
ABSENCE: no schema evolution plan or versioned row format specified
findings.finding: The append-only `criterion_firings.jsonl` ledger uses a compact short-key schema (`t`, `ts`, `r`, `v`, `c`, `n`, `u`, `d`, `s`, `p`, `x`) documented only in the harness docstring, with no schema-version field emitted per row. Because the ledger is explicitly prune-immune and long-lived (its rationale is to outlast the 50-round retention prune), any future schema change silently invalidates old rows at read time, and the `--report` logic has no way to detect or handle mixed-schema ledgers.
findings.location: Scope — files: `runs/criterion_firings.jsonl`; Context / problem: 'prune-immune ledger'; What this delivers §1: short-key table
scenarios: A future harness revision adds or renames a field; `--report` silently miscomputes metrics over the mixed-format ledger without error
A future consumer (CI job, analytics script) reads old rows expecting a field added in a newer schema version
findings.suggested_fix: Emit `"_v": 1` in every row; in `--report`, group rows by `_v`, warn (or skip) any version the current harness doesn't recognize, and document the schema version in `docs/plan-review-gate.md` rather than only in the harness docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the complete `drop_reason` value vocabulary in the plan (null = surfaced/un-floored; 'novelty' = novelty rising floor; 'completion' = completion floor) and confirm AC4(b)'s synthetic fixture uses one of the non-null values.
evidence: Plan: 'the per-firing drop_reason is still recorded to LABEL floor-driven drops for future floor-era segmentation' — references the field as a segmentation label without defining its vocabulary.
AC4(b): 'blocking fix-unit later floor-suppressed (drop_reason set)' — the test needs a concrete drop_reason value but the plan gives none.
sidecar.py:438: '"novelty" (novelty rising floor), or null (surfaced/normal)' — partial vocabulary in a code comment.
__init__.py:104: drop_reason='novelty'; __init__.py:247: drop_reason='completion' — the two non-null values live in production code, not in the plan.
findings.finding: The `drop_reason` value vocabulary is referenced in the plan as the field `x` in the ledger schema and cited as a future segmentation label ('per-firing drop_reason is still recorded to LABEL floor-driven drops for future floor-era segmentation'), but its complete value set is never enumerated in the plan. An implementer writing AC4(b) — which asserts a 'floor-suppressed (drop_reason set)' fixture — must independently discover that the valid non-null values are 'novelty' and 'completion' by reading the codebase.
findings.location: What this delivers — section 1 (Recorder), `x`=drop_reason field description; AC4(b)
scenarios: An implementer writing AC4(b) uses drop_reason='floor' or drop_reason='suppressed' in their synthetic fixture, which doesn't match what production code emits — the test passes but is not a valid reproduction of the real signal.
A future floor adds a third drop_reason value; the plan gives no guidance on whether the metric logic should treat all non-null drop_reason as floor-suppressed or only specific values.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the `x`=drop_reason field description: 'Values: null (finding was surfaced — block or advisory); "novelty" (dropped by novelty rising floor); "completion" (dropped by completion floor). AC4(b) fixture should set drop_reason="novelty" or "completion".' This mirrors the vocabulary documented in sidecar.py:438 and __init__.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a file-level exclusive lock (e.g. `fcntl.flock` / a `.lock` sidecar file) around the read-max-ts → append sequence in `--record` mode, or document that concurrent invocations are unsupported and that the caller (cron/CI) must ensure single-writer discipline.
evidence: 'the default --record mode is incremental (appends only reviews whose filename ts_ns exceeds the ledger's max recorded ts)'
'--report reads the committed ledger as-is and never auto-records'
No file-locking primitive or exclusive-open flag mentioned for the --record append path
Dedup described only for 'Re-running EITHER mode' (backfill), not for concurrent incremental runs writing simultaneously
findings.finding: The incremental `--record` path has a TOCTOU race: it reads the ledger's max `ts` and then appends new rows as two non-atomic steps. Two concurrent invocations (e.g. a slow cron run overlapping the next scheduled run, or a developer running it manually while CI also runs it) both observe the same max `ts`, both compute the same 'new' rows, and both append them — producing duplicate rows. `--report` reads the ledger 'as-is' with no dedup, so duplicate rows inflate denominators and skew `detection_proxy` / `blocking_fp_proxy` rates.
findings.location: 'What this delivers' §1 (incremental --record mode); AC2 (idempotent incremental append); 'Wiring / cadence' section
scenarios: A cron job fires every hour; the N-th run is slow (large corpus) and the (N+1)-th starts before it finishes; both append identical rows; --report double-counts them.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the incremental append path with `fcntl.flock(fd, fcntl.LOCK_EX)` on the ledger file before reading the max ts, and release after the append, ensuring only one writer at a time can complete the check-then-act sequence.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the --record incremental mode's behavior when `criterion_firings.jsonl` does not yet exist (e.g., treat missing ledger as max_ts=0 so all reviews are ingested, equivalent to a first-run backfill), and add an AC or test case covering this cold-start path.
evidence: Plan text: 'the default --record mode is incremental (appends only reviews whose filename ts_ns exceeds the ledger's max recorded ts)'
Plan text: '--backfill seeds the ledger from the entire existing corpus'
No AC or spec text addresses what --record does when criterion_firings.jsonl is absent
AC1 only proves the --backfill path; AC2 proves idempotency after a prior --backfill run — neither covers cold-start --record without prior --backfill
findings.finding: The `--record` incremental mode (non-backfill) is specified to 'append only reviews whose filename ts_ns exceeds the ledger's max recorded ts', but the plan does not specify behavior when the ledger file does not yet exist at all (e.g., a fresh checkout where --backfill has never been run). Reading the ledger's max ts from a missing file is an error state the plan leaves unspecified, so a user running `--record` before `--backfill` on a clean clone has no defined outcome (crash, silent empty-ledger creation, or confusing error).
findings.location: What this delivers — section 1 (Recorder), incremental --record description: 'default --record mode is incremental (appends only reviews whose filename ts_ns exceeds the ledger's max recorded ts)'
scenarios: Developer clones the repo, skips --backfill, runs `--record` to start incremental tracking — harness attempts to read max ts from a non-existent ledger file, crashes or silently misbehaves with no specified recovery path.
findings.suggested_fix: State in the spec: 'If the ledger file is absent, --record treats max_ts as 0 (epoch) and ingests all available sidecar reviews, creating the ledger.' Add a synthetic fixture test (AC4 or a new sub-case) asserting this behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the write strategy for `--backfill`: confirm rows are flushed incrementally (e.g., per-row or per-ticket append with explicit flush) so that a crash preserves partial progress and re-running truly resumes rather than restarts.
evidence: Plan says '--backfill seeds the ledger from the entire existing corpus'
Plan gives no write-strategy (streaming vs. batch-then-write) or flush cadence
ABSENCE: no mention of per-row or per-ticket flush, nor any checkpoint/restart mechanism for large corpora
findings.finding: The plan is silent on whether `--backfill` writes rows incrementally (streaming per-row flush to the JSONL file) or buffers all rows in memory and writes them in a single pass. If output is buffered, a crash mid-backfill leaves the ledger either empty or at a prior state, and re-running `--backfill` must re-process the entire corpus from scratch — meaning large corpora have no partial-progress preservation.
findings.location: Scope — Recorder section; 'seeds the ledger from the entire existing corpus' prose; AC1 proving command
scenarios: Corpus grows to tens of thousands of sidecar files; backfill takes minutes and dies partway
CI memory limit exceeded mid-backfill, killing the process before any rows are flushed

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the ticket-ID enumeration strategy for `--backfill` in the recorder description: state explicitly that it iterates the tracker directory (e.g., `os.listdir(tracker_dir)` filtered to subdirectories containing `*-REVIEW_RESULT.json` files) to discover all tickets with sidecar history, independent of compiled state or the existing ledger.
evidence: Plan: 'reads the persisted REVIEW_RESULT sidecars and appends one lean firing row per (review-round, finding)' — no mention of how ticket IDs are enumerated
Plan: '--backfill seeds the ledger from the entire existing corpus' — 'existing corpus' is undefined at the mechanism level
sidecar.all_review_results signature: `def all_review_results(ticket_id: str, *, repo_root=None)` — takes one ticket_id, does not enumerate tickets
ABSENCE: no mention of os.listdir(tracker_dir), reduce_all_tickets, or list_tickets in the recorder description
findings.finding: The plan never specifies how the recorder discovers which ticket IDs exist when running `--backfill`. `sidecar.all_review_results(ticket_id)` takes a single ticket ID — it does not enumerate the corpus. The backfill must iterate over some ticket-ID source (e.g., `os.listdir(tracker_dir)` filtered to directories containing `*-REVIEW_RESULT.json` files, or `rebar.list_tickets()`), but the plan is silent on this mechanism. An implementer cannot infer the discovery strategy from the plan text, and a wrong choice (e.g., iterating the ledger — which is empty on first backfill) would silently produce an empty ledger that passes AC1's `wc -l > 5000` only if the implementer already knew the right approach.
findings.location: Scope — files / Recorder section (item 1, `--backfill` description); AC1 proving command
scenarios: Implementer iterates the (initially empty) ledger for ticket IDs during --backfill, producing an empty ledger that silently fails AC1.
Implementer uses list_tickets() which only returns open/in-progress tickets, missing closed tickets with review history.
Implementer uses reduce_all_tickets() which loads all compiled state — much heavier than a simple directory scan — when os.listdir is sufficient.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to item 1 (Recorder): 'Ticket discovery for `--backfill`: iterate `os.listdir(tracker_dir)`, selecting subdirectories (skipping dot-dirs and non-dirs) that contain at least one `*-REVIEW_RESULT.json` file — the same membership predicate `sidecar.all_review_results` uses internally. This is a pure filesystem scan: no `reduce_all_tickets`, no `list_tickets`, no compiled-state load.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the approximate actual row count and criterion count observed from the existing sidecar corpus when the AC1 thresholds (>5000, ≥30) were chosen, so an executor can distinguish a code error from a data-size mismatch (e.g. add a parenthetical: 'threshold based on observed ~N rows / M criteria in the existing corpus').
evidence: 'test $(wc -l < docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/criterion_firings.jsonl) -gt 5000'
'test $(jq -r \'.c[]\' … | sort -u | wc -l) -ge 30'
No stated 'existing corpus yields ~N rows' calibration sentence anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: AC1's pass/fail thresholds (>5000 rows, ≥30 criterion ids) are asserted against the live sidecar corpus but not grounded in a stated approximate actual count from the existing corpus. If the backfill yields fewer rows than the threshold — due to a smaller-than-assumed corpus or a filtering bug — the proving command silently fails, and an executor cannot distinguish 'correct code, insufficient data' from 'logic error.' The plan should state the approximate row count / criterion count the author observed when designing the threshold.
scenarios: Executor implements the recorder correctly but the existing sidecar corpus has only 4,200 firings — AC1 fails, leaving the executor unable to tell if the threshold or the code is wrong.
A future corpus shrink (e.g. sidecar pruning) causes CI to regress on AC1 with no diagnostic.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the AC1 block: 'Threshold grounded in the existing corpus: backfill of the current sidecar yields approximately N rows covering M distinct criterion ids (observed by the plan author).' Replace N and M with the actual numbers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `--no-refresh` on `--report` should be dropped (since `--report` never auto-records by definition) rather than retained as a symmetry alias, to avoid a dead flag that misleads future users and appears load-bearing in AC3.
evidence: Plan quote: 'the `--no-refresh` flag is an explicit alias for that default (kept for symmetry with R7's `--no-refresh`)'
R7's --no-refresh prevents `subprocess.run([sys.executable, str(MINER)])` in gate_eval_instrumentation.py:189 — a real side-effect gate.
`--report` in this plan 'never auto-records' by definition, so there is no side-effect to gate, making the flag structurally dead.
AC3 proving command includes `--no-refresh` as if it were load-bearing, but its absence would produce identical behavior.
findings.finding: The `--no-refresh` flag on `--report` is a declared no-op alias copied from R7 without serving any real behavior: R7's `--no-refresh` prevents a subprocess git-walk re-mine, but `--report` never auto-records by design, so the flag is a dead toggle that silently does nothing regardless of whether it is passed. The plan acknowledges this explicitly ('kept for symmetry'), which is a cargo-cult signal — porting a flag whose rationale does not transfer.
findings.location: What this delivers — section 2 (`--report`): 'the `--no-refresh` flag is an explicit alias for that default (kept for symmetry with R7's `--no-refresh` and to make AC3's proving command self-documenting)'
scenarios: A future maintainer passes `--no-refresh` assuming it prevents some I/O, wastes time diagnosing why omitting it behaves identically.
The dead flag adds CLI surface that must be documented, tested (AC3 names it), and maintained, with zero benefit.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `--no-refresh` from the `--report` mode entirely. If symmetry with R7 is desired for discoverability, document in the help text that `--report` is always offline — no flag needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a partial-write recovery strategy for `criterion_firings.jsonl` (e.g. write rows to a temp file then `os.replace`/atomic rename into place, or add a startup step that validates and truncates any incomplete trailing line before appending).
evidence: 'append-only, prune-immune ledger runs/criterion_firings.jsonl'
'Re-running EITHER mode is idempotent (dedup key …)' — idempotency proof addresses duplicate rows, not partially-written lines
No mention of atomic write (e.g. write-to-temp + rename), line-integrity check (e.g. skip/truncate last line if incomplete), or any error-handling path for malformed ledger content
findings.finding: The append-only `criterion_firings.jsonl` ledger has no partial-write / corrupt-line recovery path. A process killed mid-append leaves an unparseable final JSON line; subsequent `--record` and `--report` invocations that read the entire ledger will fail until the file is manually repaired, silently breaking the standing recorder's core promise.
findings.location: Scope / files: `runs/criterion_firings.jsonl`; 'What this delivers' §1 (recorder design); AC2 (idempotent incremental append)
scenarios: CI or cron job is killed (SIGKILL, OOM, timeout) after partially writing a row; next scheduled run fails to parse the ledger and the recorder silently stops accumulating data.
findings.suggested_fix: In `--record` mode, accumulate new rows in-memory, write them to a sibling temp file (`criterion_firings.jsonl.tmp`), then call `os.replace(tmp, ledger)` for an atomic swap — or, for true append semantics, open in append mode but add a startup guard that reads the last line with `readline()` and truncates to the previous newline if it does not parse as valid JSON.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either note that AC prove commands require a POSIX shell (WSL/bash on Windows, or Linux/macOS CI) and document this as a prerequisite, or provide equivalent PowerShell/cross-platform prove commands alongside the POSIX ones.
evidence: AC1 prove: 'test $(wc -l < …/criterion_firings.jsonl) -gt 5000' and 'jq -r …| sort -u | wc -l'
AC2 prove: 'N=$(wc -l < …)'
AC3 prove: 'jq 'keys|length' …' and 'jq -e …'
AC5 prove: 'jq '[.[]|select(…)]|length' …'
AC6 prove: 'grep -q …'
AC7 prove: '! grep -iE …'
Windows (developer workstation) is a named platform in the support matrix; `wc`, `test`, `sort`, `jq` are absent from Windows CMD and not guaranteed in PowerShell without extra tooling
findings.finding: All seven AC prove commands rely on POSIX shell tools (`wc -l`, `jq`, `test $(...)`, `sort -u`) that are not natively available on Windows. A developer or CI runner on Windows attempting to execute the AC prove commands to verify acceptance would encounter command-not-found failures for every AC.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria AC1–AC7 prove commands, e.g. AC1: 'test $(wc -l < docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/criterion_firings.jsonl) -gt 5000' and 'jq -r '.c[]' … | sort -u | wc -l'
scenarios: A developer on a Windows workstation running CMD or PowerShell attempts the AC prove commands to verify the harness works — every AC fails with 'command not recognized' or equivalent, making it impossible to verify acceptance criteria on that platform without WSL or additional tooling.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to the AC section: 'Prove commands require a POSIX-compatible shell (Linux, macOS, or WSL on Windows). Windows users should run these in WSL or Git Bash.' This scopes the assumption without blocking the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit-test sub-case in `tests/unit/test_criterion_effectiveness.py` (or AC4) that exercises the incremental `--record` ts_ns filter: (i) a non-empty ledger with known max ts, new sidecars above and below that ts — assert only the newer rows are appended; (ii) an empty ledger — assert all rows are appended (sentinel/undefined max handled correctly).
evidence: AC2 proving command: 'python …/criterion_effectiveness.py --record --backfill' — uses --backfill for the idempotency proof, not the incremental path
AC4 lists five sub-cases (a–e) for unit tests; none mention testing the ts_ns > ledger-max filter for incremental append
Testing plan: 'The heavy detection/FP/window logic is proven on small in-memory fixtures' — no mention of incremental-append filter logic
Plan text: 'the default --record mode is incremental (appends only reviews whose filename ts_ns exceeds the ledger's max recorded ts)' — this is the primary production path
findings.finding: The incremental `--record` mode's core filter predicate — 'append only reviews whose filename `ts_ns` exceeds the ledger's max recorded `ts`' — has no unit test specified. AC2 proves idempotency only for `--backfill` re-runs (dedup key); neither AC4 nor any other AC tests that the ts_ns-based filter correctly skips already-recorded rows AND includes new ones in the incremental path. Since `--record` (without `--backfill`) is the default standing-cadence invocation, a bug in this filter could silently emit duplicates or miss new firings without any test catching it.
scenarios: A new sidecar batch arrives; --record is run; the ts_ns filter has an off-by-one (uses >= instead of >); rows are silently duplicated — no test catches this
The ledger is empty on first incremental run; max ts is undefined; the filter logic must handle this sentinel correctly — no test exercises this branch
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, add sub-case (f): synthetic fixture with a pre-populated ledger (max ts = T) and two sidecar events (ts = T-1 and ts = T+1); assert `--record` (incremental) appends exactly one row (the T+1 event) and the final ledger count is unchanged for the T-1 event.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reparent this story from R7 (b1a4) to the epic (6982-2e75-f8fa-43b9) as a direct sibling of R7, since its scope (new harness, new ledger, new metrics artifact, new test, new doc sections, detection_proxy metric) is explicitly outside R7's plan and the leaf itself describes this work as 'DISTINCT from and complementary to R7.'
evidence: Leaf states: 'DISTINCT from and complementary to R7 — not a duplicate'
Leaf states: 'a detection signal (does the criterion catch real, acted-on defects?) that R7 does not provide'

R7 scope lists exactly: gate_eval_instrumentation.py, gate_eval_metrics.json, test_gate_eval_classifier.py, and docs/plan-review-gate.md 'Blocking-FP-proxy demotion' section — no mention of criterion_effectiveness.py, criterion_firings.jsonl, criterion_effectiveness.json, test_criterion_effectiveness.py, detection_proxy, or --record/--backfill/--report
Leaf adds new files: criterion_effectiveness.py, criterion_firings.jsonl, criterion_effectiveness.json, tests/unit/test_criterion_effectiveness.py — all absent from R7's scope
Epic (6982) lists R7=b1a4 as the instrumentation story; this story is not listed as a child of R7 in the epic's integration record
findings.finding: The leaf's declared scope is NOT a subset of its parent (R7 / b1a4). R7's plan defines a single harness (gate_eval_instrumentation.py), the outcome-corpus join, specific CLI flags (--verify-repro, --emit, --window, --no-refresh), one output artifact (gate_eval_metrics.json), one test file (test_gate_eval_classifier.py), and one docs/plan-review-gate.md section ('Blocking-FP-proxy demotion'). The leaf adds an entirely new harness (criterion_effectiveness.py), a new append-only ledger (criterion_firings.jsonl), a new metrics artifact (criterion_effectiveness.json), a new test (test_criterion_effectiveness.py), a new detection_proxy metric, new --record/--backfill/--report CLI, and new doc sections — none of which appear in R7's plan. The leaf's own text confirms this is intentionally 'DISTINCT from and complementary to R7' and delivers 'a detection signal R7 does not provide.' This is an expansion beyond R7's scope, not a narrowing of it. The correct fix is to reparent this story as a direct child of the epic (6982), at the same level as R7, not as a child of R7.
findings.location: Leaf plan — 'What this delivers (DISTINCT from and complementary to R7)' section; Scope — files section; R7 parent Scope section
scenarios: A reviewer applying G7 finds the leaf's deliverables are additive beyond R7's contract, not a consistent narrowing — the parent cannot authorize scope it does not contain.
If R7 is the authoritative contract for instrumentation, future reviewers may expect this story's deliverables to be part of R7's attestation, creating confusion about what R7 actually shipped.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the ticket's parent_id from b1a4-af7a-4a48-465d (R7) to 6982-2e75-f8fa-43b9 (the epic). This accurately reflects the architectural relationship the leaf's own 'What this delivers' section describes. Optionally add a relates_to link to b1a4 to record the complementary relationship.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how a mid-write-corrupted JSONL tail is handled on re-entry (e.g., open in append mode with per-line flush so only the last line is at risk, detect and strip an incomplete final line on startup, or write to a `.tmp` file and atomically rename — and document which strategy is used).
evidence: Plan states 're-running EITHER mode is idempotent (dedup key (t, ts, r, n))'
Plan states '--backfill is also re-runnable without duplication'
No mention of tail-corruption detection, last-line truncation guard, or write-then-rename atomicity strategy
ABSENCE: no recovery procedure for a partially-written JSONL tail
findings.finding: The plan's idempotency/resumability guarantee assumes the JSONL ledger is parseable on re-entry, but does not specify how a partially-written (mid-line-corrupted) ledger is detected and recovered. A process kill mid-write leaves the last JSONL line incomplete; the next `--backfill` or `--record` invocation would encounter a JSON parse error on that truncated line and likely abort rather than resume cleanly, contradicting the stated re-runability.
findings.location: Scope — Recorder section; AC2 idempotency claim; 'dedup key (t, ts, r, n)' prose
scenarios: Process receives SIGKILL during the backfill write loop mid-line
CI runner times out partway through a large corpus backfill
Disk-full condition truncates the final JSONL record
findings.suggested_fix: On startup, before reading the ledger, scan its last line and discard it if it is not valid JSON (a one-line guard). Then document this in the harness docstring so the resume guarantee is unconditional.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
